terms.definition: Peak kilovoltage applied across the X-ray tube; controls beam energy, penetration and image contrast.
terms.exampleSentence: A higher kVp was selected to adequately penetrate the patient's large abdomen.
terms.contextNotes: Higher kVp lowers contrast but reduces dose.
commonMistakes: using too low a kVp for large patients

terms.definition: Milliampere-seconds; product of tube current and exposure time, controlling the quantity of X-ray photons produced.
terms.exampleSentence: The mAs was reduced for paediatric imaging in line with ALARA principles.
terms.contextNotes: Directly proportional to patient dose and image noise.

terms.definition: Numerical indicator of the radiation dose reaching the digital detector, used to monitor exposure adequacy.
terms.exampleSentence: A consistently high exposure index suggested over-exposure that warranted protocol review.
terms.contextNotes: Vendors define their own EI scale; check local target.

terms.definition: Range of exposures over which a detector or film produces diagnostically acceptable images.
terms.exampleSentence: Digital detectors offer wider latitude than film, reducing the need for repeat exposures.
terms.contextNotes: Wider latitude tolerates exposure errors but can mask over-exposure.

terms.definition: Ability of an imaging system to distinguish between tissues of similar attenuation or signal intensity.
terms.exampleSentence: MRI offers superior contrast resolution for soft-tissue structures compared with CT.
terms.contextNotes: Often inversely related to spatial resolution.

terms.definition: Ability of an imaging system to distinguish two adjacent small structures as separate.
terms.exampleSentence: High-resolution CT provides excellent spatial resolution for assessing interstitial lung disease.
terms.contextNotes: Measured in line pairs per millimetre.

terms.definition: Image feature that does not represent true anatomy, arising from equipment, technique or patient factors.
terms.exampleSentence: A motion artefact obscured the lung bases on the CT scan.
terms.contextNotes: Always describe artefacts and their likely cause in reports.

terms.definition: Principle of keeping radiation exposure As Low As Reasonably Achievable while obtaining diagnostic images.
terms.exampleSentence: Following ALARA, the radiographer reduced the field size and used the lowest acceptable mAs.
terms.contextNotes: ALARP (UK) is functionally equivalent to ALARA.
commonMistakes: treating ALARA as a target rather than a principle

terms.definition: Ionising Radiation (Medical Exposure) Regulations governing safe and justified medical use of ionising radiation in the UK.
terms.exampleSentence: All medical exposures must be justified and authorised under IRMER.
terms.contextNotes: Defines duty holders: employer, referrer, practitioner, operator.

terms.definition: Formal abbreviation of the Ionising Radiation (Medical Exposure) Regulations as used in UK regulatory documents.
terms.exampleSentence: The IR(ME)R practitioner authorised the CT examination after reviewing the request.
terms.contextNotes: Updated as IR(ME)R 2017 with subsequent amendments.

terms.definition: Quantity of radiation energy absorbed or imparted, measured for example in milligray or millisievert.
terms.exampleSentence: The cumulative dose from repeated CT scans was discussed with the referring clinician.
terms.contextNotes: Distinguish absorbed (Gy) from effective (Sv) dose.

terms.definition: Dose-area product; product of absorbed dose and irradiated area, used to estimate patient exposure during fluoroscopy.
terms.exampleSentence: The DAP for the angiographic procedure was recorded in the patient's notes.
terms.contextNotes: Expressed in cGy·cm² or Gy·cm²; check local convention.

terms.definition: Protective garment containing lead-equivalent material that attenuates scattered radiation to staff and patients.
terms.exampleSentence: Both the radiographer and accompanying parent wore a lead apron during the paediatric examination.
terms.contextNotes: Inspect aprons regularly for cracks and document quality assurance.

terms.definition: Personal monitoring device worn by staff to measure cumulative occupational radiation exposure.
terms.exampleSentence: Each radiographer wore a dosimeter at chest level beneath the lead apron.
terms.contextNotes: Position over the trunk and report damage promptly.
commonMistakes: wearing dosimeter outside the apron incorrectly

terms.definition: Radiation dose received by staff in the course of their work, subject to statutory dose limits.
terms.exampleSentence: Annual occupational exposure was well below the regulatory limit for classified workers.
terms.contextNotes: Classified workers subject to additional medical surveillance.

terms.definition: Iodine-based intravascular contrast agent used to enhance vascular and parenchymal structures during X-ray and CT imaging.
terms.exampleSentence: Renal function was checked before administering iodinated contrast for the CT urogram.
terms.contextNotes: Use lowest effective volume in patients with reduced eGFR.

terms.definition: Paramagnetic contrast agent used to enhance tissue signal during magnetic resonance imaging examinations.
terms.exampleSentence: Gadolinium was administered to characterise the suspected meningioma on MRI.
terms.contextNotes: Avoid in severe renal impairment due to NSF risk.

terms.definition: Insoluble radio-opaque contrast medium used for fluoroscopic imaging of the gastrointestinal tract.
terms.exampleSentence: A barium swallow was performed to assess the patient's progressive dysphagia.
terms.contextNotes: Contraindicated when GI perforation is suspected.

terms.definition: Contrast medium taken by mouth to opacify the gastrointestinal tract during imaging.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient drank dilute oral contrast one hour before the abdominal CT.
terms.contextNotes: Use water-soluble agents if perforation or aspiration risk.

terms.definition: Contrast medium injected intravenously to enhance vascular and parenchymal structures on CT and angiography.
terms.exampleSentence: IV contrast was administered via an 18-gauge cannula in the antecubital fossa.
terms.contextNotes: Confirm patent cannula and brisk flush before injection.
commonMistakes: using small-gauge cannulas at high flow

terms.definition: Specific clinical situations in which a procedure or medication should not be performed or administered.
terms.exampleSentence: Severe renal impairment and previous anaphylaxis are contraindications to iodinated contrast.
terms.contextNotes: Distinguish absolute from relative contraindications.

terms.definition: Inadvertent leakage of intravenously administered contrast medium into surrounding soft tissues.
terms.exampleSentence: Extravasation of contrast at the cannula site caused painful swelling of the forearm.
terms.contextNotes: Document volume, site and management; elevate the limb.

terms.definition: Medication given before contrast administration to reduce the risk of hypersensitivity reactions.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient received corticosteroid premedication due to a previous mild contrast reaction.
terms.contextNotes: Limited evidence for efficacy in severe reactions.

terms.definition: Use of lead-equivalent barriers to reduce radiation exposure to patients, staff and the public.
terms.exampleSentence: Gonad shielding was applied where it would not obscure the area of clinical interest.
terms.contextNotes: Routine gonad shielding guidance has changed in some jurisdictions.
commonMistakes: misplacing shield over the area of interest

terms.definition: Designated zone where access is restricted to control radiation exposure during imaging procedures.
terms.exampleSentence: Only essential personnel were permitted in the controlled area during exposure.
terms.contextNotes: Signage and warning lights must be clearly visible.

terms.definition: Secondary X-ray photons deflected from the primary beam, contributing to staff dose and reduced image contrast.
terms.exampleSentence: Standing behind the lead screen minimised exposure to scatter radiation during fluoroscopy.
terms.contextNotes: Most scatter to staff comes from the patient, not the tube.
commonMistakes: assuming the tube is the main scatter source

terms.definition: Device of lead strips placed between the patient and detector to absorb scattered photons and improve contrast.
terms.exampleSentence: An anti-scatter grid was used for the abdominal radiograph to enhance image contrast.
terms.contextNotes: Increases dose; omit in small body parts and paediatrics.

terms.definition: Mandatory checking and documentation of possible pregnancy in patients of childbearing potential before ionising imaging.
terms.exampleSentence: Pregnancy enquiry was completed and documented before the lumbar spine X-ray.
terms.contextNotes: Apply local rules such as 10-day or 28-day guidance.

terms.definition: Process of explaining a procedure's purpose, risks and alternatives so the patient can voluntarily agree to it.
terms.exampleSentence: Written informed consent was obtained before the CT-guided lung biopsy.
terms.contextNotes: Document capacity, risks discussed and patient understanding.
commonMistakes: assuming verbal consent suffices for invasive procedures

terms.definition: Overall diagnostic value of an image, determined by contrast, spatial resolution, noise and artefact.
terms.exampleSentence: Optimising image quality while minimising dose is central to radiographic practice.
terms.contextNotes: Always balance image quality against radiation dose.
